attributes.stem: A 42-year-old woman presents to the General Practice clinic with worsening symptoms of cold and colour changes in her fingers over the past year. She reports that her fingers turn white, then blue, then red when exposed to cold. Her medical history includes hypertension for which she takes amlodipine. On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits. There is skin thickening of her fingers extending to the metacarpophalangeal joints. Several small dilated blood vessels are visible on her hands. There are small pitting scars on her fingertips.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following antibodies is most likely to be positive?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with features characteristic of limited systemic sclerosis: Raynaud phenomenon (triphasic colour changes), sclerodactyly (skin thickening of fingers), telangiectasia (dilated blood vessels), and digital ulcers (pitting scars). Anti-centromere antibodies are present in 70-80% of patients with limited systemic sclerosis and are highly specific for this condition.

Anti-PM/Scl antibodies are found in patients with polymyositis/scleroderma overlap syndrome, which typically presents with muscle weakness and inflammation in addition to skin changes. These features are not present in this case.

Anti-RNA polymerase III antibodies are associated with diffuse systemic sclerosis, and generally associated with rapidly progressive skin involvement as well as an increased risk for scleroderma renal crisis. There may also be an increased risk of malignancy. This patient's presentation is more consistent with limited rather than diffuse disease.

Anti-dsDNA antibodies are highly specific for systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE). While Raynaud phenomenon can occur in SLE, the other features in this case (sclerodactyly, telangiectasia) are more typical of systemic sclerosis.

Anti-topoisomerase I antibodies (also known as anti-Scl-70) are typically associated with diffuse systemic sclerosis, and also a higher risk of severe interstitial lung disease. This patient's presentation is more consistent with limited rather than diffuse disease.

The pattern of skin involvement (limited to fingers), presence of Raynaud phenomenon, telangiectasia, and digital ulcers strongly suggests limited systemic sclerosis, making anti-centromere antibodies the most likely to be positive. The presence of these antibodies also helps predict prognosis, as limited disease generally has a better outcome than diffuse disease.
learningPoints: Anti-centromere antibodies are strongly associated with limited systemic sclerosis.
Raynaud phenomenon and sclerodactyly are characteristic features of limited systemic sclerosis.
Telangiectasia and digital ulcers are common complications of limited systemic sclerosis.
references: Adigun R, Goyal A, Bansal P, Hariz A. Systemic Sclerosis (CREST syndrome). In: StatPearls [online]. 2020. Available at: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK430875/

attributes.name: Graduating
attributes.description: Only suitable for a graduating medical student at the end of their degree
